pattern: Once sorted, an optimal pairing only ever matches small values against the largest available values, so a two-pointer sweep between the bottom half and the top half greedily maximizes how many pairs can be formed.
hints: Sort the values first. Intuitively, the smallest values should be matched against the largest ones, since a small value is the easiest partner to satisfy the doubling condition with.

Split the sorted array roughly down the middle; walk a pointer through the small half and a pointer through the large half together, only advancing the small pointer when a match against the current large value succeeds.

Every time the doubling condition holds between the current small-half and large-half candidates, that is one confirmed pair (two marked indices); walk both halves forward and count how many such pairs you can lock in.

edge_inputs.description: two elements that satisfy the doubling condition exactly
smallest and largest possible values paired together
all identical values, none can pair with each other
every element can be marked once matched optimally
duplicates of a small value cannot pair with each other, only with a large outlier
greedily pairing the very smallest with the very largest first is not always right

stress.description: uniformly random values at full length
values drawn from a small pool, stressing many equal-value ties
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    pool = sorted(rng.sample(range(1, 1000000000), 5))
    return [[rng.choice(pool) for _ in range(n)]]
